content.problemStatement:
Chef has gone to see a movie. Chef has XXX (X≥100X \ge 100X≥100) rupees with him.

The movie ticket itself costs 100100100, and Chef must buy one movie ticket to be able to see the movie.

With the money leftover, Chef will buy as many popcorn buckets as possible. A single popcorn bucket costs 505050 rupees. How many popcorn buckets will Chef be able to buy?


Input Format

The first and only line of input contains a single integer XXX.


Output Format
Output the number of popcorn buckets Chef can buy after paying for the movie ticket.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer X (100 ≤ X ≤ 250) – the amount of rupees Chef has.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single integer on its own line – the maximum number of popcorn buckets Chef can buy after purchasing the movie ticket.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Chef spends 100 rupees on the ticket, leaving 45 rupees. Since a popcorn bucket costs 50 rupees, he cannot buy any.
After paying 100 rupees for the ticket, Chef has 125 rupees left. Each bucket costs 50 rupees, so he can buy floor(125/50) = 2 buckets.

solution.approach: For each test case we first deduct the ticket price (100 rupees) from Chef's total money. The remaining amount can be spent on popcorn buckets, each costing 50 rupees. The maximum number of buckets is the integer division of the remaining money by 50, i.e., floor((X - 100) / 50). This computation is O(1) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work for each of the T test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables are used.
